coredata.dc:title: High sensitivity detection of H2O2 and glucose based on carbon quantum dots-catalyzed 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Chunling
Qin, Xiu
Xu, Yuanjin
Jing, Qingqing
Shi, Rui
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with fluorescence quantum yield of 15.8% were prepared from pig liver via hydrothermal method. These CQDs exhibited peroxidase-like activity and catalyzed the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to form oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with an absorption peak at 652 nm. The influences of acidity, temperature and incubation time on the catalytic activity of CQDs were studied. Under the conditions of pH 3.5, temperature of 40 °C, CQDs of 200 µL, TMB of 2.0 mM, and incubation time of 30 min, the increase of absorbance at 652 nm, induced by the CQD-catalyzed TMB oxidation, enabled the sensitive determination of H2O2 in the range of 5.0–60.0 µM with a low detection limit of 0.86 µM. Given that H2O2 was one of the products of glucose catalytic oxidation, a convenient colorimetric method for glucose determination was also developed. The experimental results showed that the absorbance response was strongly correlated with glucose concentration of 10.0–200.0 µM. The detection limit of glucose was calculated to be 2.89 µM. The method developed herein exhibited excellent selectivity with no interference from the matrix (such as lactose, fructose, sucrose, ascorbic acid and various amino acids). Moreover, this method was used for determining glucose in human serum with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Enzyme mimics
Colorimetry
Determination
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from pig liver
Procedures for H2O2 and glucose detection
TEM and spectroscopic characterization of CQDs

The authors thank National Natural Science Foundation of China (61664002), the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2020016), and Innovation Training Fund for college students of Guangxi University (202010593195) for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images of the prepared CQDs. (B) Particle size distribution of CQDs.
(A) The excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of CQDs. Inset: pictures of CQDs solution under daylight (left) and ultraviolet light (right). (B) FTIR spectrum of CQDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs-TMB solution containing H2O2 with different concentration. Inset: color change of corresponding solution. (B) Plot of absorbance and H2O2 concentration.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs-TMB solution containing glucose with different concentration. Inset: color change of corresponding solution. (B) Plot of absorbance and glucose concentration.
Selectivity of glucose assay, the concentration of glucose and other substances concentration were 0.1 and 1.0 mM, respectively. Inset: color change of different substance solutions.
Schematic diagram of CQDs preparation and glucose determination.
Assay of glucose in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: High sensitivity detection of H2O2 and glucose based on carbon quantum dots-catalyzed 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with fluorescence quantum yield of 15.8% were prepared from pig liver via hydrothermal method. These CQDs exhibited peroxidase-like activity and catalyzed the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to form oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with an absorption peak at 652 nm. The influences of acidity, temperature and incubation time on the catalytic activity of CQDs were studied. Under the conditions of pH 3.5, temperature of 40 °C, CQDs of 200 µL, TMB of 2.0 mM, and incubation time of 30 min, the increase of absorbance at 652 nm, induced by the CQD-catalyzed TMB oxidation, enabled the sensitive determination of H2O2 in the range of 5.0–60.0 µM with a low detection limit of 0.86 µM. Given that H2O2 was one of the products of glucose catalytic oxidation, a convenient colorimetric method for glucose determination was also developed. The experimental results showed that the absorbance response was strongly correlated with glucose concentration of 10.0–200.0 µM. The detection limit of glucose was calculated to be 2.89 µM. The method developed herein exhibited excellent selectivity with no interference from the matrix (such as lactose, fructose, sucrose, ascorbic acid and various amino acids). Moreover, this method was used for determining glucose in human serum with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is an indispensable substance and an energy source of daily activities [1–3]. The blood glucose concentration ranging from 3.0 mM to 8.0 mM can support the normal activity of tissues in the body [4,5]. The upper limit of normal blood glucose concentration is a condition known as hyperglycemia, and the lower limit is a condition called hypoglycemia [6]. The content of serum glucose in the human body is a reliable index for clinical diagnosis of diabetes [7,8]. The incidence rate of diabetes is increasing worldwide, and this condition has become a serious disease that threatens human health and wellbeing [9]. Therefore, glucose detection in serum is important in clinical practice. Several methods for glucose detection, such as electrochemistry [10,11], chromatography [12,13], fluorimetry [14,15], and colorimetry [16,17], have been developed. Each method has its advantages and disadvantages. Compared with other methods, the colorimetric method is popular because it is simple and rapid, requires inexpensive analytical instruments, and provides excellent visual detection [7]. The colorimetric determination of glucose is an indirect analytical method. Under the catalysis of glucose oxidase (GOx), glucose is oxidized to produce hydrogen peroxide which further oxidizes TMB to produce a blue ox-TMB under the catalysis of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Thus, glucose content can be indirectly determined by measuring the absorbance of the blue solution. However, HRP is a natural enzyme that is inactivated, hydrolyzed, and expensive [18]. As a result, the use of artificial or mimic enzyme has been favored because of its low cost, good stability, and easy preparation [19]. With the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, various nanomaterials with peroxidase-like activity, such as precious metals [20,21], metal oxides or sulfides [22,23], and carbon-based materials [24,25], have been used for the detection of H2O2 and glucose.

CQDs are a kind of inexpensive and nontoxic nanomaterials, widely used in chemical analysis [26], fluorescence imaging [27], and catalysis [28]. CQDs can be prepared from different carbon sources, including chemical reagents [29], animal tissues [30], plants [31], and carbon-containing wastes [32]. However, most of these CQDs have either good fluorescence performance or high catalytic performance. CQDs with both fluorescent and catalytic properties are rare [33]. The researches [34,35] showed that multi-element doping could change the internal electronic environment of CQDs, which not only improved the luminescence efficiency of CQDs, but also provided new active centers and endowed CQDs with new functions, expanding the application fields of CQDs. Considering the key role of an Fe element in mimetic peroxidase and the fact that a pig liver is rich in the Fe element, we prepared CQDs from the pig liver by a simple hydrothermal method. The obtained CQDs showed the blue fluorescence and peroxidase-like activity, which could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to form a blue ox-TMB. As H2O2 was one of the products of glucose catalytic oxidation, a method for colorimetric determination of H2O2 and glucose was thus established by combining the catalytic properties of CQDs and GOx (Scheme 1 ). This method was simple, sensitive and selective, which was employed to the determination of glucose in human serum with satisfactory results.

GOx and TMB were obtained from McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium acetate (NaAc), acetic acid (HAc), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), glucose (Glu), lactose (Lac), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc), ascorbic acid (AA), glutamic acid (Gln), valine (Val), serine (Ser), isoleucine (Ile), tryptophan (Trp), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr) and cysteine (Cys) were acquired from Damao Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China).

The UV–vis absorption spectra were achieved with a Unico 4802 spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). The morphology and size distribution of CQDs were acquired via a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements using Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN TEM (FEI Co., America) operated at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co., Ltd. Madison, WI, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from pig liver
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs with peroxidase-like activity were prepared using pig liver as the carbon precursor. 5.0 g of the cleaned pig liver was crushed and mixed with 40 mL of double deionized water (DDW) in a 75 mL Teflon Lined hydrothermal reactor, and the reactor was sealed and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reactor cooled to room temperature, the dark brown solution was purified through filtration with 0.22 μm membrane and then dialyzed against DDW for 24 h. Finally, the solution containing CQDs was collected from the dialysis bag and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for H2O2 and glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for H2O2 detection was as follows: 0.5 mL of 20 mM TMB, 0.2 mL of CQDs, and 0.5 mL of H2O2 with different concentrations were mixed in 3.8 mL HAc–NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5) and incubated in a water bath at 40 °C for 30 min. The mixed solutions were then placed in an ice-water bath for 2 min to terminate the reaction. The absorbance of the resultant solution at 652 nm was recorded for H2O2 analysis.

The glucose detection was performed as following: 0.1 mL of GOx aqueous solution (2.0 mg·mL−1) and 0.5 mL of glucose aqueous solution with different concentrations were fully mixed in 1.0 mL NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer (pH 7.0) and incubated in a 37 °C water bath for 60 min; then 0.5 mL of 20 mM TMB, 0.2 mL of CQDs and 2.7 mL HAc–NaAc buffer with pH 3.5 were successively added to the above glucose reaction system. And the follow-up procedures were carried out according to those of the H2O2 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and spectroscopic characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size distribution of CQDs were identified by TEM. As can be observed, the CQDs are almost monodispersed spheres (Fig. 1 A) with a uniform size distribution between 1.63 and 4.26 nm and an average diameter of 3.01 nm (Fig. 1B) based on measurements of 100 particles. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1A) of a single dot shows well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponded to the (100) facets of aromatic carbon framework [36], indicating that graphene microstructures formed within the CQDs during the hydrothermal process [35].

Fig. 2 A depicts the excitation-dependent emission spectra of the CQDs. As the λex increases from 300 to 440 nm, the emission peaks are red-shifted from 433 to 505 nm. The fluorescence intensity initially increases and then decreases, the maximum emission intensity is reached when excited at 360 nm. This excitation-dependent emission behavior may be related to surface defects and particle size of CQDs [37]. Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of as-prepared CQDs was measured by comparing the wavelength integrated intensity of testing sample to that of Rhodamine6G (QY = 95%). According to the calculation method from literature [38], the QY of CQDs was estimated to be 15.8%, which is higher than or close to those prepared using other carbon precursors [36,39,40].

FTIR spectra were analyzed to explore the groups of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the broad band at 3288 cm− 1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H bond. The absorption at 2964 and 1662 cm−1 belong to stretching vibrations of CH and CO groups [18], respectively. The bands at 1454 and 1241 cm−1 are attributed to COO and CN bonds [34], respectively. The absorptions at 1401 and 1110 cm−1 prove the presence of COH and COC groups in obtained CQDs [41]. These results indicate that the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups such as COOH and –OH on the surface of CQDs make them more hydrophilic and easier to disperse in aqueous solution.

Previous studies demonstrated that CQDs prepared from some carbon precursors could be used as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric analysis. On the basis of the chemical structure of CQDs, the catalytic activity could be attributed to the existence of CO group-based graphitic carbon, which had been verified to possess peroxidase-like activity [42]. In this study, such a structure within CQDs was confirmed by the HRTEM and FTIR. Control experiments were performed to further confirm the peroxidase-like catalytic property of the obtained CQDs (Fig. S1). The mixture of TMB, CQDs and HAc–NaAc buffer at pH 3.5 (hereinafter referred to as buffer) is colorless without absorption in the range of 500 to 750 nm (curve a). TMB in the buffer containing H2O2 only causes a light blue solution with a low characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm (curve b), implying that H2O2 slowly oxidizes TMB. However, TMB in the buffer containing H2O2 and CQDs forms a dark blue solution with strong absorbance at 652 nm (curve c), which is analogous to HRP-catalyzed TMB oxidation [43]. The results confirmed that the obtained CQDs showed peroxidase activity and could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2.

In order to study the kinetic mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of CQDs, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters for CQDs-catalyzed TMB oxidation were measured by varying the concentrations of TMB and H2O2. The experiments were performed by using 0.2 mL of CQDs in a buffer containing either H2O2 (0.1 mM) with TMB concentration ranging from 0.4 to 3.2 mM or TMB (2.0 mM) with various concentrations of H2O2 (0.02–0.16 mM). The absorbance at 652 nm was measured over time. Given the molar absorption coefficient of ox-TMB is 3.9 × 104 M−1·cm−1, the concentration of ox-TMB can be acquired by Beer–Lambert law. The initial velocity of reaction was calculated and then plotted against TMB or H2O2 concentration. Figs. S2A and S2B show that the CQD-catalyzed TMB oxidation by H2O2 conformed to Michaelis–Menten model for both TMB and H2O2. The Michaelis constant (K m) and maximum reaction velocity (V max) are obtained from following equation:
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
·
1
S
+
1
V max
where V represents the initial velocity; [S] stands for substrate concentration. A low K m value indicates a high catalytic activity, i.e., the lower the K m value, the stronger the affinity between enzyme and substrate will be. According to the double-reciprocal curves (Figs. S2C and S2D), the K m values of CQDs toward TMB and H2O2 are 0.6 and 0.16 mM, respectively. As summarized in Table S1, the K m value of CQDs with H2O2 as the substrate is lower than that of HRP [44], papain [45], ficin [46], MnO2 [47], AuNPs@g–C3N4 [48], and M−CQDs [33], implying that a low H2O2 concentration is needed for the CQDs to achieve the maximum reaction velocity compared with the aforementioned catalysts. Moreover, the K m value of CQDs toward TMB is larger than that of ficin, AuNPs@g–C3N4, and M−CQDs, suggesting that the CQDs have a lower affinity for TMB than for the other catalysts. The K m value of CQDs toward TMB is close to that of HRP and MoS2 [5], indicating that the affinity between the CQDs and TMB is comparable to that of HRP and MoS2. Therefore, the obtained CQDs can be used as an effective peroxidase mimic.

Similar to natural enzymes and other peroxidase mimics, the catalytic activity of CQDs is dependent on acidity of medium, incubation temperature and time. Several important experimental conditions, including pH, temperature, time, TMB and CQD concentrations, were optimized to achieve the best response. TMB oxidation usually occurs in a weak acid medium. Therefore, the catalytic activity of CQDs in the HAc–NaAc buffer (0.2 M) at pH 2.5–5.5 was studied. The absorbance of ox-TMB reached the maximum value at pH 3.5 and the absorbance decreased beyond this pH value (Fig. S3). As a result, pH 3.5 was chosen as the best acidity. The effects of temperature on CQD-catalyzed TMB oxidation were evaluated by varying the temperature of water bath from 30 °C to 70 °C (Fig. S4). In a water bath at 30 °C, the slow reaction speed did not fully oxidize TMB, thus resulting in a low absorbance value. When the water bath temperature was higher than 40 °C, H2O2 decomposition led to incomplete TMB oxidation, thus also resulting in a low absorbance. The best response was obtained at 40 °C. As the incubation time in water bath at 40 °C increased, the absorbance of ox-TMB rapidly increased, indicating that more TMB was oxidized (Fig. S5). Given that a long incubation time (more than 30 min) did not result in a substantial increase in absorbance, hence, 30 min was adopted in this study. The effects of TMB concentration and CQD dosage on the oxidation process were also investigated. The maximum absorbance was obtained at 2.0 mM TMB and 200 µL of CQDs (Figs. S6 and S7). Thus, the CQDs exhibited their maximum catalytic activity in HAc–NaAc buffer at pH 3.5 and incubation in water bath at 40 °C for 30 min with 2.0 mM TMB and 200 µL of CQDs.

H2O2 is an important active oxygen species that plays a vital part in biological activities and food manufacturing [49]. According to the principle of detection, the amount of ox-TMB generated by the system is related to H2O2 concentration. Thus, H2O2 concentration could be measured. Under optimal experimental conditions, the linear response of H2O2 was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 3 A, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually increases as H2O2 concentration increases, and the solution changes from colorless to blue (inset in Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows that the absorbance and H2O2 concentration has a good linear relation from 5.0 to 60.0 µM. The linear regression equation was A 652nm = 0.0128C H2O2 + 0.0288 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9931, and a low detection limit of 0.86 µM for H2O2 was obtained on the basis of 10σ/k. The analysis performance of this detection system was compared with that of other methods (Table S2 [50–56]). As seen, the proposed method provided both a wide linear dynamic range and a lower detection limit than the other methods. Therefore, this detection system is an effective method for sensitive H2O2 detection.

In the presence of GOx and O2, glucose can be oxidized to generate gluconic acid and H2O2. A method for the determination of glucose concentration was developed on the basis of this theory. The absorbance at 652 nm gradually increases as the dosage of glucose increases (Fig. 4 A). As can be seen in Fig. 4B, the absorbance is proportional to the glucose concentration in the range of 10.0–200.0 µM, the regression equation could be described as A 652nm = 0.0038C glucose + 0.0327 (R2 = 0.9964). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 80 µM glucose was 2.9% in five parallel measurements, thereby demonstrating the repeatability and reliability of the method. The limit of detection was 2.89 µM, which was lower than glucose concentration in human serum. The sensing performance of this method was compared with that of other methods. As summarized in Table S3 [9,13,21,46,57–60], our method was superior to most of the methods for glucose determination in linear range or sensitivity. Thus, the method developed herein can be applied to detect glucose in actual sample.

Selectivity is a crucial parameter to evaluate the performance of a proposed method. Accordingly, several substances, such as Fru, Lac, Suc, AA, Gln, Val, Ser, Ile, Trp, Pro, Thr, and Cys, which may interfere with glucose determination in serum, were examined. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the concentration of these substances was 1.0 mM and that of glucose was 0.1 mM. Only glucose induced a remarkable increase in absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. 5 ). The solution containing the interferences was nearly colorless, whereas the color of the solution containing glucose was blue (inset in Fig. 5). Owing to the specificity of GOx, other substances could not be oxidized to generate H2O2, and TMB could not be oxidized to form a chromogenic reaction. These results indicated that the approach developed herein is highly selective.

Glucose in serum was detected to check the feasibility of the proposed method. Three serum samples of healthy people were provided by members of our laboratory and taken by Guangxi University Hospital. To remove serum protein, 1.0 mL 5% trichloroacetic acid was well mixed with 1.0 mL of serum sample followed by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and diluted with NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer (pH 7.0) to 10.0 mL, and 0.5 mL of the diluted sample was then taken for glucose determination. The results are presented in Table 1 . Considering that the samples were diluted 100 times during the determination, the content of glucose in the original serum samples was 4.54, 6.41, and 6.95 mM, respectively, which was consistent with the range of 3.0 to 8.0 mM in the healthy people. On the other hand, the results of standard addition method showed that the measured values were in agreement with the added values of glucose concentration. The recoveries of glucose in serum samples ranged from 95.5% to 103.3% with RSD less than 5.0%, suggesting that this method exhibited good accuracy and precision when employed to the determination of glucose in real samples.

CQDs with QY of 15.8% were prepared from pig liver, which exhibited peroxidase-like activity and catalyzed the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to form blue ox-TMB. A simple method for colorimetric detections of H2O2 and glucose was developed using CQDs and GOx. The absorbance at 652 nm induced by the CQD-catalyzed TMB oxidation enabled the sensitive assay of H2O2 in the range of 5.00–60.0 µM. Under the optimized experimental conditions, good linear relationship between absorbance at 652 nm and glucose concentration within 10.00–200.0 μM was achieved, and low detection limit of 2.89 μM was obtained. The proposed method was used for determining glucose in human serum with satisfactory results, demonstrating the potential of the method in practical applications. CQDs as mimic peroxidase showed several advantages over noble metal, metal oxide or sulfide nanomaterials, including sufficient carbon sources, easy preparation, no toxicity and high catalytic efficiency.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank National Natural Science Foundation of China (61664002), the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2020016), and Innovation Training Fund for college students of Guangxi University (202010593195) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105365.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
